scenarios.task: For next year's strategy, I want to pursue all five of the promising opportunities the leadership team identified — a new product line, international expansion, a platform partnership, an enterprise upsell motion, and a new self-serve tier. They're all genuinely attractive and I don't want to leave good opportunities on the table.
criteria: Flags pursuing all five as the strategy-as-wish-list failure mode, naming it explicitly
States that a strategy that doesn't rule anything out isn't actually a strategy, regardless of how attractive each individual opportunity is
Recommends explicitly choosing which few things the company will focus resources on and declining or deferring the rest
Frames the real job as choosing what to say no to, not just deciding what sounds promising enough to say yes to

scenarios.task: I just announced to the whole company that cross-team collaboration is our top priority this year. Our comp and promotion criteria are still based entirely on individual output metrics per team, same as before — I don't think that needs to change since the announcement itself should shift behavior.
criteria: Flags the mismatch between the announced priority and the unchanged incentive structure, naming it as incentive-strategy mismatch
States that the organization behaves according to its actual incentive structure, not its stated priorities, regardless of how the announcement was communicated
Recommends checking and likely changing comp/promotion criteria to actually reward collaborative behavior if that's the real goal
Predicts the announced priority won't change behavior on its own while individual-output incentives remain in place

scenarios.task: Every request to change a page in our internal wiki, adjust a Slack channel's settings, or pick a new lunch vendor now has to come to me for approval, since I want to make sure I'm staying on top of everything happening in the company as we scale.
criteria: Flags this as treating clearly reversible, low-stakes decisions as one-way doors, naming the over-centralization failure mode
States that reversible decisions should be made fast and cheaply by whoever is closest to the information, not centralized through the CEO
Recommends explicitly classifying decisions by reversibility and delegating the two-way-door ones
Notes the cost of over-centralizing: it slows the organization and burns the CEO's attention on things that didn't need it

scenarios.task: My board updates and weekly leadership reports have been consistently positive for the last two quarters — every team reports being on track, no major red flags have come up through the normal reporting chain. I feel good about where things stand based on what I'm hearing.
criteria: Questions whether consistently positive reporting reflects reality or filtered information, naming the losing-the-disconfirming-signal failure mode
States that information degrades in accuracy as it travels up through layers with an incentive to soften bad news

Recommends actively seeking disconfirming signal through channels less subject to that filtering — skip-levels, direct customer contact, anonymous feedback — rather than trusting the reporting chain alone
Frames consistently good news with no dissent as itself worth investigating, not simply reassuring
